articles.title: This simple blood test might detect depression before symptoms appear
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers reported that depression may be detectable with a blood test. The study focused on accelerated aging in monocytes, a type of white blood cell. This immune-cell aging was linked more strongly to emotional and cognitive symptoms such as hopelessness and loss of pleasure than to physical symptoms like fatigue. The findings suggest a possible biological marker for depression before obvious symptoms appear.

articles.title: Weight loss drug Ozempic linked to lower depression and anxiety risk
articles.summary_4_sentences: A large study examined GLP-1 drugs such as semaglutide, sold as Ozempic and Wegovy. Researchers tracked nearly 100,000 people over more than a decade. Use of these medications was linked to fewer psychiatric hospital visits and fewer sick days. The results suggest a possible association between GLP-1 treatment and lower depression and anxiety risk.

articles.title: Alzheimer's drugs may not work and could raise brain risks
articles.summary_4_sentences: A major review assessed drugs designed to clear amyloid beta from the brain. The analysis included more than 20,000 participants. It concluded that these drugs may not provide meaningful patient benefit. The review also found increased risks of brain swelling and bleeding, including cases without obvious symptoms.

articles.title: Scientists found the brain doesn't start blank, it starts full
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers studied how early neural networks form in the hippocampus. They found that these networks begin as dense and apparently random connection webs. Over time, the brain becomes more organized by pruning connections. This result challenges the view that the brain begins as an empty framework.

articles.title: Scientists just discovered what coffee is really doing to your gut and brain
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers found that both caffeinated and decaf coffee altered gut bacteria. These changes were linked to better mood and lower stress. Decaf improved learning and memory, while caffeine improved focus and reduced anxiety. The findings suggest that coffee affects the gut and brain through more than caffeine alone.

articles.title: The creepy feeling in old buildings might have a surprising cause
articles.summary_4_sentences: A small experiment tested the effects of infrasound, which is below the range of human hearing. Participants exposed to it became more irritable and less engaged. They also showed higher cortisol levels despite not knowing the sound was present. The results suggest that inaudible low-frequency vibrations can influence mood and stress responses.

articles.title: Boosting one protein helps the brain fight Alzheimer's
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers increased the protein Sox9 to activate astrocytes in the brain. In mice with memory problems, this approach reduced plaque buildup. It also preserved cognitive function over time. The findings suggest astrocyte activation may help the brain clear harmful Alzheimer's-related material.

articles.title: Scientists discover a hidden brain "cleaning" effect triggered by movement
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers linked simple body movement to fluid circulation around the brain. They found that tightening abdominal muscles can cause subtle brain motion inside the skull. This motion appears to help move cerebrospinal fluid. The finding suggests movement may support the brain's waste-clearance processes.

articles.title: New Study on Why SuperAgers Live Longer and Have Better Memories
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers investigated why SuperAgers retain strong memory and live longer. The study identified multiple biological factors related to brain health. It also pointed to contributions from lifestyle and genetics. The findings suggest possible paths for promoting healthy aging.

articles.title: Newly mapped brain networks link far‑flung regions
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers created a detailed map of brain networks linking distant cortical regions. Advanced imaging revealed previously unknown communication pathways. The work helps explain how distributed circuits support complex cognition. It also provides new leads for studying neurological disorders.

articles.title: This hidden kind of stress may be damaging your memory as you age
articles.summary_4_sentences: A study of older Chinese Americans linked internalized stress to faster memory decline. Feelings of hopelessness were especially associated with poorer cognitive outcomes. Community support did not show the same effect in this report. The findings point to culturally sensitive stress interventions as a possible way to protect cognitive health.

articles.title: Scientists may have found the brain's switch for chronic pain
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers identified the caudal granular insular cortex as a possible control point for chronic pain. In animal studies, this region appeared to help maintain pain signals after healing. Shutting down the pathway prevented chronic pain from developing. The intervention also erased established pain in the reported experiments.

articles.title: Fish oil may be hurting your brain, new study finds
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers examined the omega-3 fatty acid EPA in people with repeated mild head injuries. They found that EPA may interfere with the brain's repair processes in this setting. The study linked it to weaker blood vessel stability, disrupted healing signals, and harmful protein buildup. The results suggest fish oil's effects on brain recovery may depend on context.

articles.title: Harvard scientists link gut bacteria to depression through hidden inflammation trigger
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers found that Morganella morganii can interact with a common pollutant to produce an inflammatory molecule. The study linked this inflammation trigger to depression-related biology. It provides a molecular example of how gut microbes may influence brain health. The work raises the possibility of treatments aimed at immune pathways rather than the brain alone.

articles.title: For the first time, scientists pinpoint the brain cells behind depression
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers analyzed donated brain tissue to study depression at the cellular level. They identified two brain cell types that behaved differently in people with depression. The altered cells included neurons linked to mood and stress and immune-related microglia. The findings reinforce that depression involves measurable biological changes in the brain.

articles.title: These 80-year-olds have the memory of 50-year-olds. Scientists now know why
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers examined adults over 80 known as SuperAgers. These individuals showed memory performance comparable to much younger people. The report linked their preservation of cognition to social lifestyles and distinctive brain biology. Scientists say these insights may help guide strategies to delay or prevent dementia.

articles.title: Suicide deaths in U.S. teens and young adults fell after 988 launch
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers compared suicide rates before and after the 988 Lifeline became operational. They found a statistically significant decline in suicide deaths among teens and young adults afterward. The pattern suggests the crisis line may offer timely support during emergencies. The authors emphasized the value of continued access to mental health resources.

articles.title: A simple blood test could reveal Alzheimer's risk years early
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists studied neutrophil levels as a potential early marker of dementia risk. Higher levels were linked to a greater chance of developing Alzheimer's disease. Because neutrophils are part of the routine inflammatory blood profile, the measure could be widely accessible. The findings also suggest immune cells may contribute directly to disease development.

articles.title: Scientists found a protein that drives brain aging -- and how to stop it
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers identified the protein FTL1 as a contributor to brain aging in mice. Higher FTL1 levels were associated with weaker connections between brain cells and memory decline. Lowering the protein restored connections and improved memory performance. The work points to FTL1 as a potential target for age-related cognitive decline.

articles.title: Protein Inhibition Found to Sensitize Aggressive Lymphoma to Therapy
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers reported that inhibiting a specific protein made aggressive lymphoma more responsive to treatment. The work focused on aggressive non-Hodgkin lymphoma models. Combining the inhibition with standard chemotherapy increased tumor cell death. The findings suggest a possible new therapeutic strategy for difficult lymphoma cases.

articles.title: Novel Combination Therapy for Metastatic Pancreatic Cancer
articles.summary_4_sentences: Investigators tested a new combination therapy for metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Preclinical results showed synergistic effects on tumor growth and survival. The regimen combined a targeted agent with immunotherapy to address resistance. The study supports moving the approach toward clinical testing.

articles.title: Pesticide exposure linked to 150% higher cancer risk in major study
articles.summary_4_sentences: A major study linked pesticide-heavy environments to substantially higher cancer risk. The reported increase reached up to 150%. Researchers suggested that mixtures of chemicals may damage cells over time even when the individual compounds are considered safe. The findings highlight concern about long-term exposure to combined pesticides.

articles.title: Hidden virus in your gut may be linked to colon cancer
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers identified a virus living inside a common gut bacterium associated with colorectal cancer. The work suggests that interactions between bacteria and their viruses may affect disease risk. It may help explain why the same microbe appears in both healthy people and cancer patients. The findings could support future screening methods for earlier risk detection.

articles.title: Scientists discover hidden "master switch" driving skin cancer growth and immune escape
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers identified the protein HOXD13 as a driver of melanoma growth and immune evasion. The protein promoted blood supply to tumors and blocked cancer-fighting T cells. Disabling HOXD13 shrank tumors and improved immune access in the reported work. The study points to a possible new treatment target in melanoma.

articles.title: This missing vitamin could stop cancer cells in their tracks
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers found that vitamin B7 supports a metabolic escape route used by some cancer cells. Without biotin, those cells lost flexibility in fuel use and stopped growing. The effect was tied to a key enzyme and was stronger with mutations in a cancer-linked gene. The study suggests a new metabolic vulnerability for cancer therapy.

articles.title: Novel Biomarker Predicts Efficacy in Patients with Cancer
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers identified a biomarker linked to treatment response in cancer patients. Higher biomarker levels correlated with better outcomes from targeted therapies. The report described potential use across multiple cancer types. The finding could improve patient stratification and personalized treatment planning.

articles.title: Scientists supercharge immune cells to destroy cancer more effectively
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers developed a way to make cancer-fighting immune cells stronger and more precise. They added signaling components that increased the cells' readiness to attack tumors. Brief drug-based suppression before use unexpectedly improved later performance. The work could help create safer and more effective next-generation cell therapies.

articles.title: A common nutrient could supercharge cancer treatment
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers found that zeaxanthin can strengthen T cells and improve immunotherapy activity. The nutrient is commonly found in vegetables and supplements. The report described it as safe and accessible, with strong potential as a treatment booster. Human trials are the next stated step.

articles.title: Scientists find hidden brain cells helping deadly cancer grow
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers studying glioblastoma found that certain brain support cells can aid tumor growth. These cells sent signals that strengthened cancer cells in lab models. Blocking that communication slowed tumor growth. The study identifies a possible vulnerability in a highly aggressive brain cancer.

articles.title: Scientists create "smart" DNA drug that targets cancer cells with extreme precision
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers created a programmable drug system made from synthetic DNA. It activates only when it detects a precise combination of tumor markers. The platform can also deliver multiple drugs at once. The work suggests a more selective way to target cancer cells while limiting damage to healthy tissue.

articles.title: Scientists reveal creatine's hidden power beyond muscle gains
articles.summary_4_sentences: The report describes creatine as a naturally produced molecule that rapidly regenerates ATP. This energy role supports not only muscles but also the brain and heart during intense activity. Supplementation can improve short bursts of physical performance. The article also notes possible benefits for memory, mood, and cognitive speed, especially in people with lower baseline levels.

articles.title: The Ratio of Fats in Diet Has a Significant Effect on Immunity
articles.summary_4_sentences: This report examined how dietary fat composition affects immune function. It found that different fat ratios altered inflammatory responses and antibody production. The findings highlight nutrition as an important factor in immune health. The study suggests that adjusting dietary fat intake could improve vaccine efficacy and resistance to disease.

articles.title: You don't need intense workouts to build muscle, new study reveals
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers reported that slow, controlled lowering movements can improve strength efficiently. The exercises required less effort than more intense workouts. The study also suggested that five minutes a day of simple movements like chair squats or wall push-ups can help. The findings support a lower-intensity approach to muscle building.

articles.title: New treatment cuts bad cholesterol by nearly 50% without statins
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers developed small DNA-based molecules to block PCSK9. By shutting down this protein, cells can absorb more LDL cholesterol from the blood. The treatment lowered bad cholesterol by nearly 50% in the report. It offers a potential alternative to statins for managing cholesterol.

articles.title: Varying Your Exercise Routine Could Add Years to Your Life
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers reported that a varied exercise routine may help extend lifespan. People who engaged in diverse physical activities showed better health markers. The study suggested that variety enhances cardiovascular and metabolic benefits. It recommended including multiple forms of exercise for longevity.

articles.title: Scientists reveal the best exercise for knee arthritis pain relief
articles.summary_4_sentences: A review of 217 trials compared exercise approaches for knee osteoarthritis. Aerobic exercise was the most effective for reducing pain and improving movement. Walking, cycling, and swimming outperformed other exercise types. The review also confirmed that exercise is a safe and essential part of treatment.

articles.title: Surprising obesity discovery rewrites decades of fat metabolism science
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers found that a key fat metabolism protein does more than release fat. The protein also helps maintain healthy fat tissue and body balance. Disrupting it caused harmful effects rather than simple fat reduction. The findings change how scientists view obesity and metabolic disease.

articles.title: Repurposed Drug Combo Shows Promise for Fatty Liver Disease
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers tested two existing drugs together in animal models of fatty liver disease. One drug is used for hypertension and the other for high cholesterol. The combination reduced liver fat and improved liver function markers. The authors said clinical trials are needed to determine whether the approach works in humans.

articles.title: This one change to your exercise routine could add years to your life
articles.summary_4_sentences: Long-term research tracked more than 100,000 people for over three decades. It found that doing a variety of physical activities was linked to lower mortality risk. The benefits appeared to level off after a certain amount of activity. The results suggest that exercise diversity may matter more than repeating the same routine.

articles.title: Two common drugs may reverse fatty liver disease, study finds
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers combined two existing drugs to treat fatty liver disease in animal models. The therapy reduced liver fat and improved liver health measures. Lower doses used together worked as well as higher doses alone. The findings are promising but still require human testing.

articles.title: Increasing heat can boost malnutrition among children
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers analyzed data from 6.5 million Brazilian children. Higher ambient temperatures were associated with more undernutrition and stunting. The effect was strongest in low-income households and resource-limited regions. The study identifies climate change as a factor worsening child health disparities.

articles.title: Scientists sound alarm as dangerous amoebas spread globally
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists warned that free-living amoebae are becoming a growing global health issue. The spread is linked to warming temperatures and outdated water systems. Some of these amoebae cause deadly infections and can shelter other harmful microbes. The report calls for stronger surveillance and improved water treatment.

articles.title: Updates on the Growing Problem of Fungal Infections
articles.summary_4_sentences: This report reviewed the rise of invasive fungal infections worldwide. It cited recent estimates of 6.5 million affected people each year. The article discussed persistent challenges in diagnosis and treatment. The authors called for more awareness and greater research funding.

articles.title: Graphene kills harmful bacteria "superbugs" but spares human cells
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers found that graphene oxide can selectively destroy bacteria while sparing human cells. The material targets a molecule found only in bacterial membranes. It was effective against drug-resistant bacteria and retained activity after repeated washing. The report also noted faster wound healing as a possible added benefit.

articles.title: How the Global Wildlife Trade Harms Humans
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article examined human health harms linked to the global wildlife trade. It highlighted risks of disease transmission and ecological disruption. The report connected wildlife markets to emerging pathogens. It urged stricter regulation to protect public health.

articles.title: How climate change may increase antibiotic resistance
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers studied how climate-related stressors affect bacterial gene transfer. Higher temperatures and drought increased horizontal transfer of resistance genes. This process can accelerate the spread of antibiotic resistance in ecosystems. The authors warned that climate change may worsen resistant infections as a public health problem.

articles.title: Scientists create plastic that destroys viruses on contact
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers developed a plastic film covered with microscopic pillars that physically damage viruses. In lab tests, it destroyed or disabled about 94% of virus particles within an hour. The approach relies on surface structure rather than chemical agents. The material could be used to make virus-resistant everyday surfaces.

articles.title: Scientists discover skincare compound that kills drug-resistant bacteria
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers found that madecassic acid from Centella asiatica can kill drug-resistant bacteria. The compound acts on a bacterial protein that humans do not have. It was reported to work against dangerous E. coli strains. The study suggests a possible new antibiotic strategy based on a skincare ingredient.

articles.title: A vaccine for Lyme disease could be on the horizon
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers advanced a Lyme disease vaccine candidate into late-stage trials. It targets Borrelia burgdorferi to prevent infection after tick bites. Other efforts are also testing antibodies and anti-tick approaches. A successful vaccine could help curb the growing incidence of Lyme disease.

articles.title: spread antibiotic resistance
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers studied gene transfer agents, which are virus-like particles used by bacteria to exchange DNA. The work showed that these particles can help spread genes, including antibiotic resistance genes. A three-gene hub called LypABC controlled cell bursting and release of the DNA carriers. The findings clarify a bacterial mechanism that could influence resistance spread.

articles.title: Reducing Flu Risk by Taking Aim at Two Proteins
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers tested a strategy of inhibiting two proteins to reduce influenza risk. Experimental data showed lower viral entry and replication with this approach. The report discussed how protein-focused antivirals might be developed from these findings. It also suggested relevance for next-generation flu vaccine design.

articles.title: Hidden weak spots in HIV and Ebola revealed with breakthrough nanodisc technology
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers developed a nanodisc platform to study viral proteins in a membrane-like setting. Using it, they identified hidden antibody interactions in HIV and Ebola that standard methods had missed. The system better mimics real viruses and improves analysis of immune recognition. The technique could accelerate vaccine development for difficult viral targets.

articles.title: Scientists finally crack mystery of rare COVID vaccine blood clots
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers explained a rare clotting disorder seen after some COVID-19 vaccines or adenovirus infections. The immune system can mistake the normal blood protein PF4 for a viral protein. That confusion triggers clotting in very rare cases. The discovery could help redesign vaccines to avoid this reaction while preserving effectiveness.

articles.title: This diet could slash cholera infections by up to 100x
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers found that certain dietary proteins can greatly reduce cholera infection in the gut. Casein from dairy and wheat gluten were especially effective in the reported work. These proteins appeared to disable a bacterial mechanism used to dominate the gut environment. The findings suggest diet may influence susceptibility to cholera.

articles.title: A drug already in trials may finally stop hepatitis E
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers identified bemnifosbuvir as a possible treatment for hepatitis E. The drug is already in trials for hepatitis C. In cells and animals, it blocked hepatitis E replication without harming healthy tissue. The findings suggest the drug could potentially be repurposed if ongoing trials are successful.

articles.title: Evolution isn't random. Scientists find the same genes used for 120 million years
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers found that butterflies and moths reused the same pair of genes for warning colors over 120 million years. The species were distantly related but evolved similar color patterns. The changes came from altered gene regulation rather than changes in the genes themselves. The study suggests some evolutionary outcomes may be more predictable than expected.

articles.title: MIT scientists finally reveal the hidden structure of a mysterious high-tech material
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers mapped the three-dimensional structure of relaxor ferroelectrics in unprecedented detail. These materials are used in technologies such as medical ultrasounds and sonar systems. The work uncovered hidden nanoscale charge patterns and challenged long-standing assumptions about their behavior. The results should help refine models used to design these materials.

articles.title: Peptides are unproven as health aids. FDA may unleash them anyway
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reports on FDA consideration of pathways that could expand access to peptide supplements. It notes that many peptide products lack strong evidence of efficacy. Experts warned that broader marketing could expose consumers to unsupported health claims. The piece emphasizes regulatory uncertainty and calls for stronger safety and efficacy standards.

articles.title: Don't toss cannabis leaves: Scientists found rare compounds with medical potential
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers identified dozens of previously unknown compounds in cannabis leaves. The work included the first evidence of rare flavoalkaloids in this part of the plant. The chemical profiles varied strongly even across a few strains. The findings reveal a broader and more complex cannabis chemistry with possible medical relevance.

articles.title: Can AI help doctors avoid missed diagnoses? A new study suggests yes
articles.summary_4_sentences: A study evaluated AI tools designed to reduce diagnostic errors in clinical care. Physicians using AI assistance achieved better detection rates and missed fewer diagnoses than standard practice. The report highlighted benefits in finding subtle signals in imaging and lab data. The authors said wider clinical integration and further research are warranted.

articles.title: This AI knew the answers but didn't understand the questions
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers reassessed an AI model called Centaur that claimed to mimic human thinking across many tasks. New analysis challenged that interpretation. The report argued the model was memorizing patterns rather than truly understanding the tasks. The findings add caution to claims that such systems replicate human cognition.

articles.title: Prenatal surgery for spina bifida may get a boost from stem cells
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers are testing stem cells to improve prenatal surgery for spina bifida. Early trials suggest stem cell grafts may support neural regeneration. The approach could reduce complications and improve long-term motor outcomes. Larger clinical studies are needed to confirm the benefits.

articles.title: A forgotten drug is giving new hope to kids with a rare disease
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers repurposed the older drug DFMO for Bachmann-Bupp syndrome, an ultra-rare genetic disorder. Early patient treatments suggested the drug may ease severe symptoms by targeting the underlying malfunction. A small number of patients have already been treated with encouraging results. Progress has been limited by regulatory and logistical barriers.

articles.title: Scientists just found a chilling way life may have begun
articles.summary_4_sentences: Experiments tested how freezing and thawing might have affected primitive cell-like structures on early Earth. Some lipid compartments fused into larger units and captured DNA more effectively depending on membrane makeup. These events could have mixed important molecules in ways relevant to early chemistry. The work proposes a freezing-thawing route that may have aided the origin of life.

articles.title: A hidden map in your nose could explain how smell works
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers mapped millions of neurons in the mouse nose to study smell organization. They found that smell receptors are arranged in overlapping stripes by receptor type rather than randomly. This structure mirrors how smell information is organized in the brain. The findings reveal a coordinated sensory map from nose to neural circuits.

articles.title: Researchers ID the First Genetic Links to Frailty
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers identified the first DNA variants associated with frailty in aging populations. The genes were linked to muscle function and inflammatory pathways. Carriers had higher risk of falls and hospitalization. The results may support earlier screening and targeted interventions.

articles.title: DNA research just rewrote the origin of human species
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers analyzed genetic data from diverse modern African groups and compared it with fossil evidence. They found support for human origins involving multiple intermingling ancestral populations rather than a single source population. These groups began diverging around 120,000 to 135,000 years ago but continued exchanging genes. The findings revise a long-standing model of early human evolution.

articles.title: Scientists stunned as bacteria rewire DNA machinery to shape cells
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers discovered that cyanobacteria repurposed a molecular system once used for DNA separation. The system now helps shape the cell itself. The finding shows that ancient molecular machinery can be adapted for entirely new functions. It also adds to understanding of bacterial cell biology and evolution.

articles.title: This simple 3-amino acid trick boosts mRNA therapy 20-fold
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers improved lipid nanoparticles by adding three common amino acids. The change increased mRNA delivery by up to 20-fold and raised CRISPR editing efficiency close to 90%. The approach works by helping cells take up the treatment more easily. Early tests showed better survival and treatment outcomes.

articles.title: Artificial neurons successfully communicate with living brain cells
articles.summary_4_sentences: Engineers printed artificial neurons that can communicate with living brain cells. The devices are flexible, low-cost, and generate lifelike electrical signals. The team demonstrated activation of living brain cells in mouse tissue. The work advances efforts to connect electronic systems with the nervous system.

articles.title: Advanced Genetic Tools ID a Novel Neurodevelopmental Syndrome
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers used advanced genetic sequencing to identify a new neurodevelopmental syndrome. The study described pathogenic variants in affected individuals. The discovery expands the known genetic causes of neurodevelopmental disorders. The authors suggested integrating such tools into diagnostic workflows.

articles.title: Ultrafast MRI uncovers brain signal direction: New scan may help decode autism, Alzheimer's and hallucinations
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers used an imaging technique to identify whether brain signals in rats were moving in feedforward or feedback directions. The work distinguished bottom-up visual input from top-down expectations and predictions. The results were reported in Nature Communications. The method could help study brain changes relevant to autism, Alzheimer's, schizophrenia, hallucinations, and related conditions.

articles.title: Over 75% of Older Adults Have Used 'Complementary Therapies'
articles.summary_4_sentences: A survey found that more than three-quarters of older adults in the United States have used complementary therapies. Reported approaches included massage, yoga, cannabis, and psychedelics. The findings show broad interest in alternative health practices among seniors. The study called for greater integration of these therapies into standard care.

articles.title: Know the legal age to buy tobacco products in the U.S.? Many parents don't
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers surveyed parents about U.S. tobacco age laws. Only 45% correctly identified 21 as the legal purchase age for cigarettes, e-cigarettes, and nicotine products. Knowledge gaps were larger among parents with lower education levels. The findings suggest a need for stronger public education on tobacco regulations.

articles.title: The dirtiest thing in public bathrooms isn't the toilet seat
articles.summary_4_sentences: The report reviewed hygiene risks in public restrooms. It noted that toilet seats are often cleaner than high-touch surfaces such as door handles and flush levers. It also highlighted toilet plumes from lidless flushing as an underappreciated source of germ spread. Poor hand hygiene was identified as another major risk factor.

articles.title: Aging well in a digital world
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article discusses technology as a response to rapidly aging populations in Australia and elsewhere. It notes that digital tools are often promoted to support safe and independent living for older adults. Experts cited in the piece warned that technology alone is not sufficient. The report emphasizes that broader supports are needed alongside innovation.

articles.title: Hip replacement beats exercise-only care for moderate-to-severe hip arthritis in yearlong trial
articles.summary_4_sentences: A yearlong trial compared total hip arthroplasty plus conservative care with conservative care alone for hip osteoarthritis. Surgery produced better pain relief, function, and quality-of-life outcomes. The conservative treatment was based on an exercise program. The study concluded that hip replacement led to the best outcomes for moderate-to-severe disease.

articles.title: Artificial saliva made from sugarcane protein protects teeth from acid and decay
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers created an artificial saliva using the sugarcane protein CANECPI-5. The protein binds to enamel and forms a protective barrier against acid. In early tests, it worked even better when combined with fluoride and xylitol. The product may be especially helpful for patients who lose saliva production after cancer treatment.

articles.title: This 5-day diet helped Crohn's patients feel better fast
articles.summary_4_sentences: A clinical trial tested a fasting-mimicking diet in people with Crohn's disease. Participants followed five days a month of very low-calorie, plant-based meals. Most reported symptom improvement, and inflammatory markers also decreased. The study suggests diet may offer meaningful relief in a condition with limited dietary guidance.

articles.title: Are your memories real? Physicists revisit the Boltzmann brain paradox
articles.summary_4_sentences: A new analysis revisited the Boltzmann brain paradox in physics. The study argued that some reasoning about time and entropy relies on circular assumptions. It raised the possibility that memories and perceived reality could, in theory, arise from random fluctuations. The work primarily reframes a foundational philosophical problem in cosmology.

articles.title: Cows' methane burps may be fueled by a newfound organelle in gut microbes
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers identified a new organelle in cattle gut microbes. Laboratory work suggested that it boosts microbial feed metabolism and methane production. The finding could inform new ways to reduce livestock greenhouse gas emissions. Scientists suggested that targeting the organelle may lessen the environmental impact of cattle farming.

articles.title: Scientists finally solve mystery of strange "golden orb" found 2 miles deep
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers investigated a mysterious golden object discovered deep in the Gulf of Alaska. Microscopy, DNA sequencing, and deep-sea analysis were used to identify it. The object was not an egg or sponge but tissue from a giant deep-sea anemone. The finding resolved a long-running question about the specimen's origin.

articles.title: Mezcal worm in a bottle DNA test reveals a surprise
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers used DNA testing to identify larvae found in mezcal bottles. All sampled worms were agave redworm moth caterpillars rather than a mixture of species. The result resolves a long-standing question about what the larvae are. The work also raises concerns about sustainability as demand for mezcal and edible larvae grows.

articles.title: Giant prehistoric insects didn't need high oxygen after all, study finds
articles.summary_4_sentences: A new study challenged the idea that ancient giant insects required high oxygen levels. Researchers found that insect flight muscles were not constrained by oxygen in the expected way. Their breathing system had room to expand, so oxygen alone cannot explain giant size. The work shifts attention to other explanations such as predators or body limits.

articles.title: Scientists just found what keeps plant cells from growing out of control
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers studied the protein PEX11 in plant peroxisomes during early growth. They found that it helps control both division and size of these organelles. Altering key genes caused peroxisomes to grow abnormally large. A yeast version of the protein corrected the defect, suggesting a conserved mechanism across species.

articles.title: Giant, kraken-like octopuses may have ruled the Cretaceous deep
articles.summary_4_sentences: Paleontologists described fossil evidence for very large octopus-like cephalopods from the Cretaceous deep sea. The specimens suggest animals comparable in length to modern giant squids. Their anatomy implies they were apex predators in ancient marine ecosystems. The discovery changes views of cephalopod evolution and deep-sea diversity in the Mesozoic.

articles.title: Scientists find perfect fossils in rust beneath Australian farmland
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists uncovered exceptionally preserved fossils at McGraths Flat in New South Wales. The site preserves remains in iron-rich sediment rather than typical fossil-bearing rocks. Tiny iron particles captured fine details such as insect organs, fish eye pigments, and spider hairs. The fossils provide a detailed look at a rainforest ecosystem from 11 to 16 million years ago.

articles.title: 289-million-year-old mummified reptile reveals how breathing began on land
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers studied a mummified reptile fossil identified as Captorhinus aguti. The specimen revealed the earliest known rib-powered breathing system. This breathing method later characterized reptiles, birds, and mammals. The finding clarifies an important adaptation that helped vertebrates thrive on land.

articles.title: Ancient mass grave reveals how a pandemic wiped out a city 1,500 years ago
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers confirmed a mass grave in ancient Jordan linked to the Plague of Justinian. Hundreds of victims appeared to have been buried within days. The burial pattern showed that normally dispersed populations were suddenly concentrated in death. The site offers archaeological evidence of how pandemics can rapidly reshape communities.

articles.title: How Astronauts Relearn Grip Control in Zero Gravity
articles.summary_4_sentences: A JNeurosci study examined how astronauts relearn grip control in zero gravity. The report described effects of space travel on brain and physical responses. Results showed adaptation challenges in microgravity. The findings may help improve training for future space missions.

articles.title: _Beyond Inheritance_ offers a new view of mutations
articles.summary_4_sentences: The article reviews a book by Khamsi about mutations. It presents mutations as dynamic processes that can influence evolution and disease throughout life. The discussion includes examples of somatic mutations contributing to adaptation and illness. The book argues against viewing mutations as only harmful or static.

articles.title: Fungus from NASA cleanrooms could survive Mars conditions
articles.summary_4_sentences: The title reports that a fungus from NASA cleanrooms may survive Mars-like conditions. The available summary provides no study details, methods, or findings. Because the source text is limited, the report cannot be reliably summarized beyond the headline claim. This item would normally require fuller information for confident interpretation.

articles.title: Life on Mars? Tiny cells just survived shock waves and toxic soil
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers exposed yeast cells to simulated Martian shock waves and perchlorate salts. Some cells survived both stresses in the lab. Their survival depended on protective molecular clusters that shielded critical cellular functions. The work suggests one possible strategy by which life could persist under Mars-like conditions.

articles.title: Unusual airborne toxin detected in the U.S. for the first time
articles.summary_4_sentences: Scientists detected toxic MCCPs in air samples in the Western Hemisphere for the first time. The report pointed to fertilizer made from sewage sludge as a likely source. The finding identifies a previously unrecognized route of environmental contamination. It raises concern about airborne exposure to these compounds in the United States.

articles.title: A 67-year-old "crazy" theory about vitamin B1 has finally been proven
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers stabilized a highly reactive molecule in water, confirming a decades-old theory related to vitamin B1. The result resolves a long-standing chemical question. The work also suggests implications for greener chemical manufacturing. The study links fundamental chemistry with potential industrial applications.

articles.title: Dragonflies can see a color humans can't and it could change medicine
articles.summary_4_sentences: Researchers found that dragonflies can detect very deep red light near the infrared range. The ability depends on a specialized visual protein similar to one used in human eyes. The report suggests this helps dragonflies detect mates in flight. The discovery may also have implications for medical applications based on light detection.
